critic_raw:
Assessor A: Rare-event classification at 1.4% prevalence requires class-specific metrics. Overall accuracy is dominated by the non-defect class. The paper reports 99.1% vs 98.6% — essentially the null model's performance range. No defect recall, no precision at threshold, no false negatives per million parts. The deployment claim is unsupported. Verdict: critique_wins.

Assessor B: Two problems compound each other. First, the metric cannot detect defect-class improvements. Second, the paper explicitly states asymmetric costs but evaluates symmetrically. A paper that recognizes escape costs are dominant but then uses a metric that weights escapes and false positives equally has a fundamental methodological inconsistency. Verdict: critique_wins.

Assessor C: The management decision (replace visual rule before product launch) is being made on a metric that is essentially uninformative for defect detection quality. This is a high-stakes manufacturing quality decision. The evidence base does not support it. Report recall, precision at threshold, and false negatives per million parts. Verdict: critique_wins.

Synthesis: Unanimous critique_wins. Same three issues identified by all three assessors.
adjudication_raw: Unanimous critique_wins. Consistent with run 1. No ETD applicable — critique is structural.
